title: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period
Description: This chapter includes conditions that have their origin in the perinatal period even though death or morbidity occurs later.
Exclusions: Congenital gonococcal infection; Certain infectious or parasitic diseases - acquired after birth; Gastroenteritis or colitis of infectious origin; Hereditary haemolytic anaemia; Transient hypogammaglobulinaemia of infancy; Certain congenital diseases of the nervous system; congenital cardiomyopathy; Paralytic ileus; Pemphigus neonatorum; Cradle cap; Congenital malformations, deformations and chromosomal abnormalities; Endocrine, nutritional or metabolic diseases; Injury, poisoning or certain other consequences of external causes; Neoplasms; Retinopathy of prematurity

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal conditions that may be unrelated to present pregnancy
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to conditions associated with the mother which are unrelated to the present pregnancy.
Exclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal complications of pregnancy; fetus and newborn affected by maternal endocrine and metabolic disorders; Fetus or newborn affected by noxious influences transmitted via placenta or breast milk

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal hypertensive disorders
Description: Maternal hypertensive disorders (chronic hypertension, preeclampsia, eclampsia, preeclampsia superimposed on chronic hypertension, or gestational hypertension).
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal hypertensive disorders; fetus or newborn affected by unspecified maternal hypertension; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal gestational hypertension with significant proteinuria; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal eclampsia; pre-eclampsia affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal gestational hypertension without significant proteinuria; Fetus or newborn affected by pre-existing maternal hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium; Fetus or newborn affected by pre-existing maternal hypertensive disorder with superimposed proteinuria; Pre-eclamptic albuminuria affecting fetus or newborn; severe pre-eclamptic albuminuria affecting fetus or newborn; Pre-eclamptic proteinuria affecting fetus or newborn; Toxaemia of pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by gestational oedema or proteinuria without hypertension
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by gestational oedema or proteinuria without hypertension; Fetus and newborn affected or suspected to be affected by pregnancy-induced oedema and proteinuria without hypertension

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal renal or urinary tract diseases
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to conditions in the mother associated with the kidneys or urinary tract.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal renal or urinary tract diseases; fetus or newborn affected by other maternal diseases of urinary system; Cystitis complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Kidney abscess complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Tubular kidney necrosis complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Kidney infection complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Nephritis complicating pregnancy, childbirth or puerperium, affecting fetus or newborn; Pyelitis complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Pyelonephritis complicating pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal infectious diseases
Description: A condition affecting fetuses or newborns, that is (or suspected to be) caused by a maternal infection with a bacterial, viral, fungal, or parasitic source.
Exclusions: Infections of the genital tract in pregnancy
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal infectious diseases; Gonorrhoea complicating pregnancy, childbirth or puerperium, affecting fetus or newborn; Maternal rubella affecting fetus or newborn; Syphilis complicating pregnancy, childbirth or puerperium, affecting fetus or newborn; Tuberculosis complicating pregnancy, childbirth or puerperium, affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by periodontal disease in mother
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by periodontal disease in mother

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal respiratory diseases
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal respiratory diseases

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal nutritional disorders
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to conditions in the mother that are directly or indirectly associated with a lack of essential nutrients in the diet.

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal malnutrition
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal malnutrition

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal overweight or obesity
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal overweight or obesity; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal obesity with body mass index, adult 30 to less than 40; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal obesity with body mass index, adult equal to or greater than 40

title: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormal maternal chemistry
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormal maternal chemistry

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal injury
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to conditions in the mother resulting from physical damage or harm.
Inclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal injury, poisoning or certain other consequences of external causes
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal injury; Fetus or newborn affected by maternal injury, poisoning or certain other consequences of external causes

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal chemotherapy
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal chemotherapy

title: Fetus or newborn affected by surgical procedure on mother
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to conditions in the mother resulting from surgical health intervention.
Exclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by caesarean delivery; damage to placenta from amniocentesis, caesarean section or surgical induction; previous surgery to uterus or pelvic organs; Termination of pregnancy, affecting surviving fetus or newborn
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by surgical procedure on mother

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal anaemia
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal anaemia; Anaemia complicating pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn; Anaemia of pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal complications of pregnancy
Description: Any other condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to any condition of the mother due to an adverse evolution (complication) which may arise associated with the time period from conception through childbirth.

title: Fetus or newborn affected by incompetence of cervix uteri
Description: Cervical incompetence refers to a weakness of the cervix and lower uterine segment, which can lead to recurrent second-trimester or early third-trimester loss of pregnancy due to an inability of the uterine cervix to retain a pregnancy until term. It is associated with a premature shortening of the cervix, dilatation, and opening of the cervical os during pregnancy. Ultrasound changes that suggest cervical incompetence include a cervical length < 1.5 cm, cervical width > 3 cm, and an expanded cervical canal > 8 mm.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by incompetence of cervix uteri; Patent cervix complicating pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by premature rupture of membranes
Description: Preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) refers to a patient who is at less than 37 weeks’ gestation and has presented with a rupture of membranes prior to the onset of labour. Complications include pre-term delivery, ascending infection, umbilical cord prolapse, oligohydramnios, placental abruption, retained placenta, postpartum haemorrhage, or rupture of the vasa praevia.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by premature rupture of membranes; premature rupture of membranes affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by oligohydramnios
Description: Oligohydramnios is defined as a decrease in the volume of amniotic fluid. It is diagnosed if the diameter of the largest amniotic fluid depot is < 2 cm, or if the amniotic fluid index (AFI) is < 5 cm.
Exclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by premature rupture of membranes
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by oligohydramnios; oligohydramnios affecting fetus

title: Fetus or newborn affected by polyhydramnios
Description: Polyhydramnios is defined as an abnormally large volume of amniotic fluid within the uterus. An amount of 2 litres at term, any single pool >8cm or an amniotic fluid index (AFI) > 24 cm is considered to be polyhydramnios.
Inclusions: fetus or newborn affected by hydramnios
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by polyhydramnios; fetus or newborn affected by hydramnios

title: Fetus or newborn affected by ectopic pregnancy
Description: An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a pregnancy begins outside of the uterus. The most common site is within one of the fallopian tubes, although in rare cases, ectopic pregnancies can occur in the stomach region, cervix, or ovary. It is often caused by a condition that slows or blocks the movement of a fertilised egg through the fallopian tube to the uterus. Ectopic pregnancies cannot continue to term and the developing cells must be removed to prevent rupture of the ectopic area, which can lead to shock and danger the life of the mother.
Inclusions: Abdominal pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by ectopic pregnancy; Abdominal pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn; fetus or neonate affected by ectopic pregnancy

title: Fetus or newborn affected by multiple pregnancy
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to any condition associated with the presence of a multiple pregnancy.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by multiple pregnancy; Fetus or newborn affected by twin pregnancy; fetus or neonate affected by twin pregnancy; Triplet pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Quadruplet pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn; Quintuplet pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Sextuplet pregnancy affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal death
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to death of the mother.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal death

title: Fetus or newborn affected by malpresentation before labour
Description: Malpresentations are all presentations of the fetus other than the vertex, and includes breech, transverse, shoulder, compound, face, and brow presentations. They may pose risks to the fetus and mother and may necessitate operative vaginal or caesarean delivery, or other interventions to accomplish delivery. Breech presentation, the most common malpresentation, results when the fetal buttocks, legs, feet, or a combination of these presents first into the maternal pelvis.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by malpresentation before labour; abnormal presentation before labour, affecting fetus or newborn; fetus affected by malpresentation; fetus or neonate affected by malpresentation before labour; malpresentation before labour affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or neonate affected by breech presentation before labour; Breech presentation before labour affecting fetus or neonate; fetus or newborn affected by breech presentation before labour; Breech presentation, with external version before labour, affecting fetus or newborn; Version with extraction, affecting fetus or newborn; Unstable lie of fetus or newborn, before labour; unstable lie before labour; Fetus or newborn affected by transverse lie before labour; Fetus or newborn affected by face presentation before labour; Fetus or newborn affected by external version before labour

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal blood loss
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal blood loss; fetus and newborn affected by accidental haemorrhage; Antepartum haemorrhage affecting fetus or newborn; Antepartum coagulation defect with haemorrhage, affecting fetus or newborn; Early haemorrhage in pregnancy, affecting fetus or newborn; Haemorrhage complicating delivery, affecting fetus or newborn; Haemorrhage with coagulation defect, affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by complications of placenta
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to an adverse evolution (complication) associated with the placenta, umbilical cord, or chorioamniotic membranes.

title: Fetus or newborn affected by placenta praevia
Description: Placenta praevia exists when the placenta lies wholly or in part in the lower segment of the uterus. Diagnosis has evolved from the clinical I-IV grading system, and is determined by ultrasonic imaging techniques relating the leading edge of the placenta to the cervical os. Grade I is a low lying placenta, Grade II is a placenta that meets the edge of the cervical os, Grade III is a placenta that partially covers the os, and Grade IV is a placenta that completely covers the os.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by placenta praevia; fetus or neonate affected by placenta praevia; Transient neonatal neutropaenia due to placental insufficiency, including maternal HELLP syndrome

title: Fetus or newborn affected by placental oedema or large placenta
Description: A large placenta, also known as placentomegaly, is one that weighs > 750 g. Placentomegaly can be seen in the following conditions: fetal hydrops, maternal diabetes mellitus, Rh incompatibility, chronic infections (e.g. syphilis, cytomegalovirus), maternal anaemia, or acute placental oedema with acute chorioamnionitis.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by placental oedema or large placenta

title: Fetus or newborn affected by placental infarction
Description: Placental infarction is the formation of localised areas of ischemic villous necrosis, usually due to vasospasm of the maternal circulation. The affected regions of the placenta are incompetent, and lead to placental insufficiency if the infarcts are severe.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by placental infarction

title: Fetus or newborn affected by placental insufficiency or small placenta
Description: Placental insufficiency is defined as the inability of the placenta to deliver a sufficient supply of oxygen and nutrients to the fetus, and therefore, is unable to sustain the growth of the developing baby until term. Placental insufficiency can result in intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), pre-eclampsia, abruption, or preterm labour and delivery. A small placenta is defined as a placenta that weighs less than the lower limit of normal for the gestational period. A low placental weight can be the result of a maternal or fetal conditions. Among maternal conditions belong those causing underperfusion of the placenta, such as pre-eclampsia or maternal hypertension. Among fetal conditions belong fetal malformations or chromosomal anomalies.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by placental insufficiency or small placenta; Fetus or newborn affected by placental insufficiency

title: Fetus or newborn affected by placental transfusion syndromes
Description: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) occurs in monozygotic twins while they are in the uterus. It occurs when blood travels from one twin to the other, and the twin that loses blood is the donor twin, while the twin that receives blood is the recipient twin. Depending on the severity of the transfusion, both infants may experience problems, such as anaemia, paleness, and dehydration in the donor twin, and redness and an increased blood pressure in the recipient twin.
Inclusions: Placental and cord abnormalities resulting in twin-to-twin or other transplacental transfusion
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by placental transfusion syndromes; Placental and cord abnormalities resulting in twin-to-twin or other transplacental transfusion; fetal transfusion syndrome; placental transfusion in fetus or newborn; placental transfusion syndrome in fetus or newborn; twin-transfusion syndrome in fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by complications of umbilical cord
Fetus or newborn affected by prolapsed cord
Description: A prolapsed umbilical cord is when the cord enters the opening cervix and down into the birth canal during labour before the baby has left the uterus. The risk of prolapse is higher if the baby is lying in a transverse position, the mother has had more than one baby, an excess amount of amniotic fluid exists, there is preterm prelabour rupture of membranes, or if membranes are artificially ruptured.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by prolapsed cord; Compression of umbilical cord with cord prolapse affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by other compression of umbilical cord
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due obstruction of blood flow through the umbilical cord secondary to pressure from an external object or misalignment of the cord itself not classified elsewhere.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by other compression of umbilical cord; Compression of umbilical cord affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by cord around neck; Fetus or newborn affected by cord tightly around neck; Fetus or newborn affected by entanglement of cord; umbilical cord entanglement, affecting fetus or newborn; umbilical cord strangulation, affectng fetus or newborn; Torsion of umbilical cord in fetus or newborn; Twisted umbilical cord in fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormalities of umbilical cord length
Fetus or newborn affected by short umbilical cord
Description: An umbilical cord < 2 SD in length below mean for the gestational age. At term, this is < 35 cm. Often associated with fetal hypokinesia
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by short umbilical cord

title: Fetus or newborn affected by long umbilical cord
Description: An umbilical cord > 2 SD in length above mean for the gestational age. At term, this is > 80 cm.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by long umbilical cord

title: Fetus or newborn affected by vasa praevia
Description: An obstetric complication characterised by fetal vessels crossing or running in close proximity to the internal orifice of the cervix (inner cervical os).
Exclusions: Fetal blood loss from vasa praevia
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by vasa praevia

title: Fetus or newborn affected by traumatic injury of the umbilical cord
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by traumatic injury of the umbilical cord; Newborn affected or suspected to be affected by umbilical cord puncture; Newborn affected or suspected to be affected by umbilical cord rupture; Newborn affected or suspected to be affected by varix umbilical cord; Newborn affected or suspected to be affected by disruption of the umbilical cord

title: Fetus or newborn affected by other abnormalities of membranes
Fetus or newborn affected by chorioamnionitis
Description: Chorioamnionitis is an infection of the placental tissues and amniotic fluid. It can lead to bacteraemia in the mother, which is an infection of the blood, and this can cause preterm birth or infection in the newborn. Organisms which are usually responsible for chorioamnionitis include Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Group B streptococcus.
Exclusions: Infections of the fetus or newborn
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by chorioamnionitis; Fetus or newborn affected by amnionitis; fetus or neonate affected by amnionitis; Fetus or newborn affected by membranitis; fetus or neonate affected by membranitis; Fetus or newborn affected by placentitis

title: Fetus or newborn affected by amniotic band syndrome
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by amniotic band syndrome

title: Fetus or newborn affected by certain complications of labour or delivery
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to any other adverse evolution (complication) during labour and delivery.

title: Fetus or newborn affected by breech delivery or extraction
Description: Breech presentation refers to a fetus that is lying with its bottom downwards. There are three different types of breech presentation: breech with extended legs (frank), fully flexed legs (complete), or footling (incomplete) with one or both thighs extended. Breech presentation is associated with an increased risk of intrapartum trauma or asphyxia, and caesarean section is a common mode of delivery to reduce birth-related complications.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by breech delivery or extraction; breech extraction affecting fetus; breech extraction affecting fetus or newborn; breech presentation, causing obstructed labour, affecting fetus or newborn; fetus affected by breech delivery; fetus or neonate affected by breech delivery and extraction; fetus or newborn affected by breech delivery and extraction

title: Fetus or newborn affected by other malpresentation, malposition or disproportion during labour or delivery
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to abnormal positions of the vertex of the fetal head (malposition) or any presentation position of the fetus other than vertex of the fetal head, first (malpresentation) during labour and delivery.

title: Fetus or newborn affected by forceps delivery
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to assisted birth in which intervention assistance is provided with smooth metal instruments curved to fit around the head (forceps).
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by forceps delivery

title: Fetus or newborn affected by delivery by vacuum extractor
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to assisted birth in which intervention assistance is provided with a soft or hard plastic or metal cup attached by a tube to a suction device that fits firmly onto the head and attaches with suction (vacuum extractor, ventouse).
Inclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by delivery by ventouse
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by delivery by vacuum extractor; Fetus or newborn affected by delivery by ventouse

title: Fetus or newborn affected by caesarean delivery
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to delivery via a surgical procedure in which one or more incisions are made through a mother's abdomen (laparotomy) and uterus (hysterotomy) to deliver one or more babies (Caesarean delivery).
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by caesarean delivery; fetus or neonate affected by caesarean section

title: Fetus or newborn affected by precipitate delivery
Description: A precipitate delivery is one that is < 3 hours and where contractions are unusually severe. It commonly occurs in multiparous women or when labour has been induced. Due to the force and speed of delivery, trauma may occur to the mother and newborn. The mother may suffer from haemorrhage, perineal laceration, infection, or uterine rupture, and the newborn may suffer from subdural hematoma, anoxia, or fractures.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by precipitate delivery; fetus or newborn affected by rapid second stage of labour; precipitate labour affecting fetus or newborn; rapid second stage affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormal uterine contractions
Description: Abnormal uterine contractions can either be hypertonic or hypotonic. Hypertonic contractions are ones that occur more frequently and are marked by an increase in resting tone to more than 15 mm Hg. Hypotonic contractions are ones where the number of contractions is unusually low, the resting tone of the uterus is less than 10 mm Hg, and the strength of contractions is consistently < 26 mm Hg.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormal uterine contractions; Arrested active phase of labour affecting fetus or newborn; Atony of uterus during labour affecting fetus or newborn; Desultory labour affecting fetus or newborn; Dyscoordinate labour affecting fetus or newborn; Dystocia of cervix affecting fetus or newborn; Dystocia of contraction ring affecting fetus or newborn; Failure of cervix dilatation in labour affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by hypertonic, incoordinate or prolonged uterine contractions; Fetus or newborn affected by hypertonic labour; hypertonic labour affecting fetus or newborn; hypertonic uterus affecting fetus or newborn; Incoordinate labour affecting fetus or newborn; uterine incoordination affecting fetus or newborn; Prolonged uterine contractions in labour affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by primary inadequate contractions; Fetus or newborn affected by uterine inertia; uterine inertia affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by secondary uterine inertia; Fetus or newborn affected by other uterine inertia; Functional disturbance of uterus complicating delivery affecting fetus or newborn; Incomplete, poor or slow cervix dilatation affecting fetus or newborn; Fetus or newborn affected by contraction ring; ring contraction affecting fetus or newborn; Uterus retraction ring affecting fetus or newborn; Irregular labour affecting fetus or newborn; Poor contraction during labour affecting fetus or newborn; Protracted labour affecting fetus or newborn; Spasm of cervix complicating delivery affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by abnormality in fetal intrauterine heart rate or rhythm
Fetus and newborn affected by abnormality in fetal intrauterine heart rate or rhythm before onset of labour
Index Terms: Fetus and newborn affected by abnormality in fetal intrauterine heart rate or rhythm before onset of labour

title: Fetus and newborn affected by abnormality in fetal intrauterine heart rate or rhythm during labour
Index Terms: Fetus and newborn affected by abnormality in fetal intrauterine heart rate or rhythm during labour

title: Meconium passage during delivery
Description: Meconium passage by the fetus during labour and/or delivery process.
Exclusions: Neonatal aspiration of meconium; Meconium staining
Index Terms: Meconium passage during delivery; Fetus and newborn affected or suspected to be affected by meconium passage during delivery

title: Fetus or newborn affected by noxious influences transmitted via placenta or breast milk
Description: A group of conditions characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any harmful or poisonous substance to the fetus or newborn via the placenta or in breast milk.
Inclusions: nonteratogenic effects of substances transmitted via placenta
Exclusions: congenital malformations; Neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia due to drugs or toxins transmitted from mother

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal anaesthesia or analgesia in pregnancy, labour or delivery
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of anaesthesia or analgesia provided to the mother during the period of time between conception and childbirth.
Inclusions: Reactions and intoxications from maternal opiates or tranquillizers administered during labour or delivery
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal anaesthesia or analgesia in pregnancy, labour or delivery; Reactions and intoxications from maternal opiates or tranquillizers administered during labour or delivery; sedation during labour or delivery affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of tobacco
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any substances derived from tobacco use by the mother to the fetus or newborn.
Exclusions: Exposure to tobacco smoke in the perinatal period
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of tobacco; maternal tobacco use, affecting fetus

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of alcohol
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any substances derived from alcohol use by the mother to the fetus or newborn.
Exclusions: Fetal alcohol syndrome
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of alcohol; Alcoholism complicating pregnancy, childbirth or puerperium affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of drugs of addiction
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any substances derived from other drug use by the mother to the fetus or newborn.
Exclusions: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal anaesthesia or analgesia in pregnancy, labour or delivery; withdrawal symptoms from maternal use of drugs of addiction
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of drugs of addiction

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of nutritional chemical substances
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any substances derived from nutritional chemical use by the mother to the fetus or newborn.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal use of nutritional chemical substances

title: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal exposure to environmental chemical substances
Description: A condition characterised by findings in the fetus or newborn due to the transmission of any substances derived from exposure of the mother to environmental chemicals.
Index Terms: Fetus or newborn affected by maternal exposure to environmental chemical substances

title: Neonatal dermatoses due to maternal antibodies
Description: A range of antibody-mediated neonatal skin disorders due to transplacental transfer of maternal antibodies to the fetus. The relevant autoimmune disorder may or may not be apparent in the mother.
Index Terms: Neonatal dermatoses due to maternal antibodies

title: Neonatal lupus erythematosus
Description: Neonatal lupus erythematosus results from trans-placental transfer of maternal antibodies, in particular anti-Ro/SSA and anti La/SSB. It manifests with an erythematous rash which may be obviously photosensitive and is closely associated with congenital heart block. The mother may have known lupus, especially subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, but she may be asymptomatic. The rash normally subsides within the first few months of life.
Index Terms: Neonatal lupus erythematosus

title: Neonatal pemphigus
Description: Neonatal pemphigus vulgaris is a short lived autoimmune skin disease arising as a result of transplacental transmission to the neonate of maternal antibodies. Neonatal pemphigus is characterised by blister formation on the skin and the mucous membranes mediated by auto-antibodies to the desmosome component desmoglein 3.
Index Terms: Neonatal pemphigus

title: Disorders of newborn related to slow fetal growth or fetal malnutrition
Small for gestational age
Description: Birth weight below – 2 standard deviations of the mean or below the 10th percentile according to local intrauterine growth charts
Inclusions: Small-for-dates

title: Small for gestational age, symmetrical
Description: Growth of the fetus is affected in early pregnancy and growth is slow throughout the duration of the pregnancy. The head circumference is proportional to the rest of the body. Birth weight is 2 standard deviations below the mean, or below the 10th percentile.
Index Terms: Small for gestational age, symmetrical

title: Small for gestational age, asymmetrical
Description: This growth restriction leads to a disparity in length and head circumference when compared to birth weight. This condition typically occurs in the third trimester.
Index Terms: Small for gestational age, asymmetrical

title: Intrauterine growth restriction
Description: The fetus does not achieve its predicted genetic potential and infant, not light or small for gestational age, showing signs of fetal malnutrition.

title: Asymmetrical intrauterine growth restriction
Description: There is restriction of body weight followed by length with general head sparing. This condition occurs late in pregnancy and is caused by extrinsic factors. Fetal malnutrition leading to low ponderal index less than 2 (weight to length ratio) but weight not severe enough to qualify as small for gestational age.
Index Terms: Asymmetrical intrauterine growth restriction; Asymmetrical IUGR

title: Symmetrical intrauterine growth restriction
Description: This condition begins earlier in pregnancy and there is a higher incidence of permanent neurologic sequela. It is often associated with either genetic abnormalities or fetal infection, especially 1st trimester viral infections.
Index Terms: Symmetrical intrauterine growth restriction; Symmetrical IUGR; symmetrical growth retardation

title: Intrauterine growth restriction associated with small for gestational age
Description: These infants are classified as small for gestational age but have also been subject to intrauterine growth restriction.
Index Terms: Intrauterine growth restriction associated with small for gestational age; fetal malnutrition without mention of expected weight for gestational age

title: Fetal intrauterine malnutrition without mention of small for gestational age
Description: Neonate, not light or small for gestational age, showing signs of fetal malnutrition, such as dry, peeling skin or loss of subcutaneous tissue.
Exclusions: fetal malnutrition with mention of: light for gestational age; fetal malnutrition with mention of: small for gestational age
Index Terms: Fetal intrauterine malnutrition without mention of small for gestational age; fetal malnutrition NOS; intrauterine malnutrition; intrauterine or fetal malnutrition; poor fetal nutrition

title: Disorders of newborn related to short gestation or low birth weight, not elsewhere classified
Description: Infants whose weight is appropriate for their gestational ages are termed appropriate for gestational age (AGA). Infants that are heavier than expected are large for gestational age (LGA). Conversely, those smaller than expected are considered small for gestational age (SGA).
Exclusions: Disorders of newborn related to slow fetal growth or fetal malnutrition
Index Terms: Disorders of newborn related to short gestation or low birth weight, not elsewhere classified

title: Extremely low birth weight of newborn
Description: Newborn birth weight 999 g or less. Infants have increased morbidity including neurosensory disability, cerebral palsy, retinopathy of prematurity, deafness, pulmonary immaturity, chronic lung disease and subnormal cognitive function.

title: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 499g or less
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing 499 g or less.
Index Terms: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 499g or less

title: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 500-749g
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing between 500 and 749 g.
Index Terms: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 500-749g

title: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 750-999g
Index Terms: Extremely low birth weight of newborn, 750-999g

title: Very low birth weight of newborn
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing between 1000 and 1499 g.

title: Very low birth weight of newborn, 1000-1249g
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing between 1000 and 1249 g.

title: Low birth weight of newborn
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing between 1500 and 2499 g.

title: Low birth weight of newborn, 2000-2499g
Description: A paediatric condition in which the infant is born weighing between 2000 and 2499 g.
Index Terms: Low birth weight of newborn, 2000-2499g

title: Extreme prematurity of newborn
Description: Less than 28 completed weeks (less than 196 completed days) of gestation.

title: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age less than 22 completed weeks
Description: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age less than 22 weeks, 0 days
Index Terms: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age less than 22 completed weeks; Extreme immaturity of newborn, less than 22 completed weeks

title: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 22 completed weeks
Description: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 22 weeks, 0 days through 22 weeks, 6 days.

title: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 23 completed weeks
Description: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 23 weeks, 0 days through 23 weeks, 6 days
Index Terms: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 23 completed weeks; Extreme immaturity of newborn, 23 completed weeks

title: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 24 completed weeks
Description: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 24 weeks, 0 days through 24 weeks, 6 days
Index Terms: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 24 completed weeks; Extreme immaturity of newborn, 24 completed weeks

Description: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 27 weeks, 0 days through 27 weeks, 6 days
Index Terms: Extreme prematurity of newborn, gestational age 27 completed weeks; Extreme immaturity of newborn, 27 completed weeks; Extreme immaturity of newborn, less than 28 weeks

Description: Preterm: is less than 37 completed weeks (less than 259 days) of gestation.

Description: Preterm newborn, gestational age 29 weeks, 0 days through 29 weeks, 6 days

Description: Preterm newborn, gestational age 30 weeks, 0 days through 30 weeks, 6 days

Description: Preterm newborn, gestational age 33 weeks, 0 days through 33 weeks, 6 days

Description: Preterm newborn, gestational age 36 weeks, 0 days through 36 weeks, 6 days

title: Disorders of newborn related to long gestation or high birth weight
Description: Usually implies gestation > 290 or 294 days (42 weeks); high birthweight = >4000g.
Index Terms: Disorders of newborn related to long gestation or high birth weight

title: Exceptionally large newborn
Description: An exceptionally large baby is defined as having a weight at birth of > 4500 g, regardless of gestational age at birth.
Exclusions: Syndrome of infant of a diabetic mother, type 1 or 2, nongestational, insulin dependent; Syndrome of infant of mother with gestational diabetes
Index Terms: Exceptionally large newborn; Birth weight 4500 grams or more; Oversize fetus; excessively large fetus or infant; macrosomia

title: Large newborn for gestational age
Description: A birth weight greater than the 90th percentile for gestational age or birth weight 4000-4499 g at term regardless of period of gestation.
Exclusions: Syndrome of infant of mother with gestational diabetes; Syndrome of infant of a diabetic mother, type 1 or 2, nongestational, insulin dependent
Index Terms: Large newborn for gestational age; Birth weight 4000g to 4499g; Other fetus or infant heavy- or large-for-dates regardless of period of gestation

title: Post-term newborn
Description: A condition of the newborn characterised by a gestational period that reached or exceeded 42 completed weeks (294 days or more) of gestation.
Exclusions: Postmaturity syndrome
Index Terms: Post-term newborn; Postmaturity NOS; hypermaturity of newborn; post-term infant; post-term newborn, not heavy for gestational age; postdate newborn; Ballantyne-Runge syndrome

title: Postmaturity syndrome
Description: Post-term infant with signs of dysmaturity including dry peeling wrinkled skin, yellow staining of the skin, long stained fingernails, abundant scalp hair, thin growth retarded body with long thin limbs and hyperalert behaviours.
Exclusions: Post-term newborn
Index Terms: Postmaturity syndrome

title: Birth injury to central nervous system
Description: A condition characterised by the presence of damage to the central nervous system due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.

title: Intracranial laceration or haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: A group of conditions characterised as a traumatic brain injury occurring when the tissue of the brain is mechanically cut or torn and bleeds in a newly delivered child due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.
Exclusions: Intracranial nontraumatic haemorrhage of fetus or newborn; intracranial haemorrhage of fetus or newborn: due to anoxia, hypoxia, or ischaemia

title: Subdural haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Haemorrhage into the subdural space (between the dura and the arachnoid) resulting from traumatic tearing of the bridging veins and venous sinuses due to rotational movement of the brain secondary to a traumatic delivery.
Exclusions: subdural haemorrhage accompanying tentorial tear
Index Terms: Subdural haemorrhage due to birth injury; localised subdural haematoma due to birth injury; subdural haematoma in fetus or newborn due to birth injury; subdural haemorrhage in fetus or newborn due to birth injury

title: Cerebral haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Cerebral haemorrhage due to birth injury refers to haemorrhage occurring into the cerebral parenchyma as a result of birth trauma, and is commonly accompanied by extracerebral contusion and/or bleeding haemorrhage in the scalp. May have associated skull fracture(s).
Index Terms: Cerebral haemorrhage due to birth injury; brain haemorrhage in fetus or newborn due to birth injury; cerebral haemorrhage due to birth trauma; cerebral haemorrhage in fetus or newborn due to birth injury; intracerebral haemorrhage in fetus or newborn due to birth injury; Cerebral laceration during birth, with haemorrhage; brain laceration due to birth injury; laceration of brain during birth, with haemorrhage

title: Cerebellar haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Haemorrhage into the cerebellum, hemispheres or vermis, due to trauma. Occipitaloseodiastasis with breech delivery is the most common cause of this injury.
Index Terms: Cerebellar haemorrhage due to birth injury

title: Intraventricular haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Traumatic haemorrhage into the intraventricular space as the dominant lesion, usually in a term infant, a result of birth trauma. Usually seen in conjunction with other intracranial bleeding (parenchymal, subdural, subarachnoid) and can be seen in the term or preterm infant.
Index Terms: Intraventricular haemorrhage due to birth injury; intraventricular haemorrhage of fetus or newborn due to birth injury

title: Subarachnoid haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Haemorrhage within the subarachnoid space (the area between the arachnoid membrane and the pia matter) due to either leakage from the leptomeningeal plexus or rupture of bridging veins within the subarachnoid space.
Index Terms: Subarachnoid haemorrhage due to birth injury; subarachnoid haematoma of fetus or newborn due to birth injury; subarachnoid haemorrhage of fetus or newborn due to birth injury

title: Tentorial tear due to birth injury
Description: Lacerations of tentorium cerebelli due to birth trauma, usually resulting in infratentorial haemorrhages; lesser degrees of tentorial injury most commonly associated with subdural bleeds.
Index Terms: Tentorial tear due to birth injury; tentorial tear; tentorial tear due to birth trauma

title: Cerebellar contusion due to birth injury
Description: A bruise of the brain tissue. There is a punctuate haemorrhage which occurs in the long gyri.
Index Terms: Cerebellar contusion due to birth injury

title: Cerebral contusion due to birth injury
Description: A bruise of the brain tissue. Focal region of necrosis and haemorrhage, usually involving the cerebral cortex and subcortical white matter
Index Terms: Cerebral contusion due to birth injury

title: Extradural or epidural haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Haemorrhage in the plane between the skull bone and the periosteum on the inner surface of the skull from injury to the middle meningeal artery from birth trauma
Index Terms: Extradural or epidural haemorrhage due to birth injury; Extradural haemorrhage due to birth injury

title: Cerebral oedema due to birth injury
Description: Cerebral oedema is an excessive accumulation of water in the intracellular or extracellular spaces of the brain. There is a breakdown of the tight endothelial junctions which make up the blood brain barrier.
Index Terms: Cerebral oedema due to birth injury; oedema of brain of fetus or newborn due to birth injury; Increased intracranial pressure due to injury at birth

title: Birth injury to spine or spinal cord
Description: Injury to the spinal cord incurred during delivery from excessive traction or rotation, principally occurring during breech and rotational forceps during vertex deliveries
Index Terms: Birth injury to spine or spinal cord; birth spinal injury; Birth injury to meninges and spinal cord; Birth spinal cord injury; Fracture of spine due to birth injury; Haematomyelia of fetus or newborn

title: Birth injury to brainstem
Description: Injury to the brain stem occurring during delivery due to excessive longitudinal traction especially when this traction is combined with flexion and torsion of the spine during delivery.
Exclusions: Fracture, dislocation or subluxation of spine due to birth injury
Index Terms: Birth injury to brainstem

title: Birth injury to eye
Description: Ocular injuries due to birth trauma include lid lacerations, hyphema, rupture of Descemet's membrane of cornea, vitreous haemorrhage, corneal oedema, abrasions and lacerations, orbital haemorrhage and fractures, and intraocular haemorrhages.
Inclusions: Subconjunctival haemorrhage due to birth injury; Traumatic glaucoma due to birth injury
Index Terms: Birth injury to eye; Ocular hypertension due to birth injury; Subconjunctival haemorrhage due to birth injury; Traumatic glaucoma due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to scalp
Description: A condition characterised by the presence of damage to the scalp due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.

title: Bruising of scalp due to birth injury
Description: Erythema of the scalp occurring usually as a result of dystocia or application of forceps
Index Terms: Bruising of scalp due to birth injury

title: Cephalohaematoma due to birth injury
Description: Cephalhaematoma is a subperiosteal collection of blood caused by rupture of vessels beneath the periosteum and does not extend across the suture lines.
Index Terms: Cephalohaematoma due to birth injury; cephalohaematoma of fetus or newborn; Cephalohaematoma deformans of Schüller

title: Chignon due to birth injury
Description: Chignon is a temporary swelling (oedema of the scalp) secondary to the placement of a ventouse suction cup used in assisted vacuum deliveries. It usually disappears after several hours
Index Terms: Chignon due to birth injury

title: Monitoring injury of scalp of newborn
Description: Injuries to the scalp from use of intrapartum fetal monitoring devices including injury following fetal blood sampling.
Index Terms: Monitoring injury of scalp of newborn; Injury to scalp of newborn due to sampling incision; Sampling incision of scalp of newborn; Injury to scalp of newborn due to scalp clip or electrode; fetal scalp electrode injury

title: Subgaleal epicranial subaponeurotic haemorrhage due to birth injury
Description: Subgaleal haemorrhage is a collection of blood in the space between the epicranial aponeurosis of the scalp and the periosteum of the skull.
Index Terms: Subgaleal epicranial subaponeurotic haemorrhage due to birth injury; epicranial subaponeurotic haemorrhage due to birth injury; subgaleal haematoma due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to skin or soft tissues
Description: Superficial injury including abrasions, lacerations and ecchymoses sustained during birth to sites other than scalp face or external genitalia.
Exclusions: Birth injury to scalp

title: Birth injury to sternocleidomastoid
Description: Injury to the sternomastoid muscle due to birth usually presents with torticollis (tilt and rotation of head) and a firm, spindle-shaped, immobile mass in the midportion of sternomastoid muscle.
Index Terms: Birth injury to sternocleidomastoid; Sternomastoid injury due to birth injury; Sternomastoid haematoma due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to external genitalia
Description: Injuries of external genitalia such as oedema, ecchymoses, or haematomas of scrotum or labia majora, haematocoele, or trauma to the testes as a result of trauma during birth, especially after a breech delivery.
Index Terms: Birth injury to external genitalia; Birth injury to penis; Haematoma of penis due to birth injury; Birth injury to scrotum; Superficial haematoma of scrotum due to birth injury; Birth injury to testes; Testicular haematoma due to birth injury; haematoma of testis, birth injury; Birth injury to vulva; Vulval oedema, ecchymosis, or haematoma due to birth injury; Haematoma of vulva of fetus or newborn

title: Subcutaneous fat necrosis due to birth injury
Description: Subcutaneous fat necrosis is a rare acute transient hypodermatitis that develops within first weeks of life in term infants.
Exclusions: Subcutaneous fat necrosis of the newborn
Index Terms: Subcutaneous fat necrosis due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to face
Description: Birth injuries to face include injuries sustained to nose (deviations, deformities, or septal damage), ears (abrasions, lacerations, deformities or haematomas).
Index Terms: Birth injury to face; Birth injury to skin and soft tissues of face, ears or neck; Facial congestion due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to peripheral nervous system
Description: A condition characterised by the presence of damage to the nerves or ganglia outside of the brain or spinal cord due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.

title: Birth injury to cranial nerves
Description: Birth injuries to the cranial nerves include contusion, avulsion, rupture, neuroma or praxis.

title: Birth injury to facial nerve
Description: Facial palsy involving both upper and lower halves of the face caused by traumatic compression of the facial nerve as it exits the stylomastoid foramen, or as it passes over the ramus of the mandible.
Index Terms: Birth injury to facial nerve; facial nerve injury due to birth trauma; injury of facial nerve, newborn; Facial palsy due to birth injury; facial paralysis due to birth injury; newborn facial nerve paralysis; newborn facial palsy; newborn facial paralysis; paralysis of facial nerve due to birth injury; Geniculate ganglionitis in newborn

title: Brachial plexus palsy in newborn
Description: Brachial plexus birth palsy occurs when the brachial plexus are damaged during birth. It occurs most typically during a difficult delivery.

title: Erb paralysis
Description: Erb paralysis is one of the most common brachial plexus birth palsy. The injury occurs in the upper brachial plexus nerves and affects the upper arm.
Index Terms: Erb paralysis; Duchenne paralysis due to birth injury; Erb palsy; Erb palsy as birth trauma; Erb palsy or paralysis, due to birth trauma; Erb paralysis due to birth injury; Erb-Duchenne palsy as birth trauma; Erb-Duchenne paralysis; brachial plexus palsy limited to C5-C6, birth injury; Erb's paralysis due to avulsion; Erb's paralysis due to neuroma; Erb's paralysis due to rupture; Erb's paralysis due to neuropraxis; Neuropraxia

title: Klumpke paralysis
Description: Klumpke paralysis is a form of brachial plexus palsy that causes paralysis in the hand.
Index Terms: Klumpke paralysis; Klumpke paralysis due to birth injury; Klumpke-Déjerine paralysis; brachial plexus palsy limited to C8-T1; Klumpke paralysis due to avulsion; Klumpke paralysis due to neuroma; Klumpke paralysis due to rupture; Klumpke paralysis due to neuropraxis

title: Phrenic nerve paralysis due to birth injury
Description: Birth injury to the cervical roots 3 to 5 resulting in the paralysis of the ipsilateral diaphragm usually after a difficult breech delivery
Index Terms: Phrenic nerve paralysis due to birth injury; phrenic nerve birth injury

title: Birth injury to skeleton
Description: A condition characterised by the presence of damage to the skeleton due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.
Exclusions: Birth injury to spine or spinal cord

title: Fracture of skull due to birth injury
Description: Linear or depressed fractures of skull bones resulting from injury during birth, including those related to forceps or vacuum assisted delivery.

title: Linear skull fracture due to birth injury
Description: Linear fractures of skull bones resulting from injury during birth, including those related to forceps and vacuum assisted delivery.
Index Terms: Linear skull fracture due to birth injury

title: Depressed skull fracture due to birth injury
Description: Depressed fractures of skull bones resulting from injury during birth, including those related to forceps and vacuum assisted delivery
Index Terms: Depressed skull fracture due to birth injury

title: Occipital osteodiastasis due to birth injury
Description: Occipital osteodiastasis (OOD) is a form of birth injury characterised by a tear along the innominate (posterior occipital or supraoccipital-exoccipital) synchondrosis with separation of the occipital squama from the lateral or condylar parts of the occipital bone.
Index Terms: Occipital osteodiastasis due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to facial bones
Mandibular bone fracture due to birth injury
Index Terms: Mandibular bone fracture due to birth injury

title: Nasal bone fracture due to birth injury
Index Terms: Nasal bone fracture due to birth injury

title: Birth injury of thorax
Description: Fracture of bones of the thorax including ribs, sternum due to birth injury.
Index Terms: Birth injury of thorax

title: Fracture, dislocation or subluxation of spine due to birth injury
Description: A true fracture of the spine is very rare due to birth injury. A fracture is usually in the cervical area and follows a difficult footling breech vaginal delivery. Dislocation refers to the displacement of one of the cervical vertebra relative to another in the cervical spine. The trauma allows one vertebra to shift position over the vertebra below. If this dislocation is partial the term “subluxation” is used. The injury to the spine may be unilateral or bilateral. Dislocations may be stable or unstable referring to the degree of mobility and the extent of additional damage to the supporting ligaments.
Index Terms: Fracture, dislocation or subluxation of spine due to birth injury

title: Fracture of clavicle due to birth injury
Description: This is a greenstick fracture of the clavicle that may occur during the birthing process.
Index Terms: Fracture of clavicle due to birth injury; fracture of clavicle due to birth trauma; fracture; clavicle, birth injury

title: Birth injury to long bones
Index Terms: Birth injury to long bones; Fracture femur due to birth injury; Fracture humerus due to birth injury; Fracture radius or ulna due to birth injury; Fracture tibia or fibula due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to other organs
Description: A group of conditions characterised by the presence of damage of organs of a newly delivered child due to physical pressure or injury during delivery.
Index Terms: Birth injury to other organs

title: Birth injury to liver
Description: Rupture or subcapsular haemorrhage into the liver parenchyma as a result of birth trauma usually seen in large for gestational age infants, those with hepatomegaly, those born by breech delivery; may present as haemoperitoneum
Index Terms: Birth injury to liver; Haematoma of liver due to birth injury; Rupture of liver due to birth injury

title: Birth injury to spleen
Description: Rupture or subcapsular haemorrhage into spleen as a result of birth trauma; may present as haemoperitoneum
Inclusions: Rupture of spleen due to birth injury
Index Terms: Birth injury to spleen; Rupture of spleen due to birth injury; congenital rupture of spleen; spleen injury due to birth trauma; splenic birth injury

title: Adrenal haemorrhage due to birth injury
Index Terms: Adrenal haemorrhage due to birth injury

title: Sepsis of fetus or newborn
Exclusions: Sepsis without septic shock; Sepsis with septic shock
Index Terms: Sepsis of fetus or newborn; congenital sepsis; neonatal sepsis; perinatal sepsis; sepsis neonatorum; Sepsis of fetus or newborn due to streptococcus, group A; neonatal sepsis due to streptococcus, group A; Sepsis of fetus or newborn due to streptococcus, group B; neonatal sepsis due to streptococcus, group B; Sepsis of fetus or newborn due to Staphylococcus aureus infection; neonatal sepsis due to Staphylococcus aureus infection; Sepsis of fetus or newborn due to Escherichia coli infection; neonatal sepsis due to Escherichia coli

title: Other bacterial infections of the fetus or newborn
Exclusions: Neonatal conjunctivitis or dacryocystitis; Neonatal infectious mastitis; Neonatal urinary tract infection; Omphalitis of newborn; Neonatal meningitis

title: Congenital tuberculosis
Description: A disease affecting infants, caused by an infection with the bacteria Mycobacterium tuberculosis in utero. Transmission is by vertical transmission.
Index Terms: Congenital tuberculosis; congenital necrotic tuberculosis; congenital tuberculous degeneration; congenital tuberculous gangrene; congenital tuberculous infection

title: Neonatal listeriosis
Description: A condition affecting fetuses or neonates, caused by an infection with the gram-positive bacteria Listeria. This condition is characterised by respiratory distress in the neonate, by stillbirth, or by abortion. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of Listeria in the neonate and mother.
Index Terms: Neonatal listeriosis; Neonatal disseminated listeriosis; congenital listeria monocytogenes infection; congenital listeriosis; fetal listeriosis

title: Viral infection in the fetus or newborn
Description: Any condition affecting fetuses or newborns, caused by an infection with a virus.

title: Congenital Zika virus infection
Index Terms: Congenital Zika virus infection

title: Congenital Epstein-Barr virus infection
Description: There are several forms of Epstein–Barr virus infection. Infectious mononucleosis, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, and Burkitt's lymphoma can all be caused by the Epstein–Barr virus.
Index Terms: Congenital Epstein-Barr virus infection

title: Congenital Varicella Zoster virus infection
Description: Transplacentally acquired Varicella zoster virus infection. Both the gestational age at the time of maternal infection and the time interval between maternal infection and birth have major influences on the clinical course.
Inclusions: Fetal varicella syndrome
Index Terms: Congenital Varicella Zoster virus infection; congenital VZV - [Varicella Zoster virus] infection; Fetal varicella syndrome; Varicella embryopathy; Disseminated perinatal varicella; Mucocutaneous perinatal varicella; Congenital varicella; congenital chickenpox

title: Congenital cytomegalovirus infection
Description: A condition affecting neonates, caused by an infection with cytomegalovirus in utero. This condition is characterised by jaundice, low birth weight, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, or pneumonia if symptoms develop shortly after birth, or may be asymptomatic. This condition commonly presents later in life with loss of hearing, loss of vision, or developmental disabilities. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by detection of cytomegalovirus in neonatal urine, saliva, blood, or other body tissues within 2-3 weeks of birth.
Index Terms: Congenital cytomegalovirus infection; congenital CMV infection

title: Congenital echovirus infection
Description: A disease affecting neonates, caused by an infection with enteric cytopathic human orphan (ECHO) virus in utero. This disease presents with various symptoms depending on the site of the infection, or may be asymptomatic. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of ECHO virus in the neonate.
Index Terms: Congenital echovirus infection

title: Congenital enterovirus infection
Description: Congenital viral infections with enteroviruses (including coxsackie viruses and ECHO viruses) are infectious embryofetopathies that have been reported to cause fetal malformations, acute systemic illness in the newborn and long-term neurodevelopmental abnormalities.
Index Terms: Congenital enterovirus infection; Congenital Coxsackie virus infection

title: Congenital human immunodeficiency virus infection
Description: A disease affecting neonates, caused by an infection with human immunodeficiency virus in utero. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of human immunodeficiency virus in the neonate.
Index Terms: Congenital human immunodeficiency virus infection; congenital AIDS; congenital HIV - [human immunodeficiency virus] infection

title: Congenital parvovirus syndrome
Description: Fetal parvovirus syndrome is a fetopathy likely to occur when a pregnant woman is infected by parvovirus B19. Fetal parvovirus infection results in aplastic crisis. Anaemia induces a risk of hydrops and fetal death by cardiac failure in 10 to 20% of cases.
Index Terms: Congenital parvovirus syndrome

title: Congenital rubella syndrome
Description: A disease caused by an infection with the rubella virus in utero. This disease presents with symptoms depending on the timing of infection of the fetus and may present with birth defects (such as hearing loss), or intrauterine growth retardation. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of rubella virus or detection of anti-rubella virus IgM antibodies in the neonate or infant.
Index Terms: Congenital rubella syndrome; Gregg syndrome; Congenital pneumonia due to Rubella; Congenital rubella pneumonitis

title: Congenital viral hepatitis
Description: A disease of the liver affecting the neonate, caused by an infection with either hepatitis A, B, C, D, or E virus in utero. This disease is characterised by lethargy, jaundice, abdominal distention, failure to thrive, or clay coloured stools. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of the hepatitis A, B, C, D, or E virus in a blood sample from the neonate.
Index Terms: Congenital viral hepatitis; congenital hepatitisvirus infection; Acute congenital viral hepatitis; Congenital Hepatitis B virus infection; Congenital hepatitis due to other specified viruses; Congenital Hepatitis C virus infection

title: Perinatal Herpes simplex infection
Description: Herpes simplex infection acquired during the perinatal period, normally from active herpes infection of the mother's genital tract, but may also be transmitted in utero.
Index Terms: Perinatal Herpes simplex infection; HSV - [herpes simplex virus] congenital infection; HSV - [herpes simplex virus] perinatal infection; congenital Herpes simplex infection; herpes neonatorum; intrauterine Herpes simplex infection

title: Fungal infection of fetus or newborn
Description: Any condition affecting fetuses or newborns, caused by an infection with a fungal agent.

title: Malassezia infection in newborn
Description: A condition affecting newborns, caused by an infection with Malassezia that leads to a severe systemic inflammatory response. This condition is characterised by fever or respiratory distress. Confirmation is by identification of Malassezia in a blood sample.
Index Terms: Malassezia infection in newborn

title: Neonatal aspergillosis
Description: A disease affecting neonates, caused by an infection with the fungi Aspergillus. This disease presents with clinical symptoms depending on the site of infection. Transmission is by inhalation of Aspergillus spores, or direct contact. Confirmation is by identification of Aspergillus from affected sites.
Index Terms: Neonatal aspergillosis

title: Neonatal candidosis
Description: A condition affecting neonates, caused by an infection with the fungi Candida. This condition is characterised by apnoea, thrombocytopenia, or decreasing respiratory function or other symptoms depending on the site of infection. Transmission is vertical (from the mother to the baby). Confirmation is by identification of Candida in a blood or urine sample.
Index Terms: Neonatal candidosis; congenital candidiasis; congenital candidosis; neonatal candida infection; neonatal candidiasis; neonatal monilia; neonatal monilia infection; neonatal moniliasis; neonatal thrush; Invasive neonatal candidosis; invasive neonatal candidiasis; Prenatally-acquired mucocutaneous candidosis; congenital cutaneous candidosis; congenital mucocutaneous candidosis

title: Parasitic diseases in the fetus or newborn
Description: Any condition affecting fetuses or newborns, caused by an infection with a parasite.
Exclusions: Congenital syphilis; Necrotising enterocolitis of newborn; Tetanus neonatorum

title: Congenital toxoplasmosis
Description: A disease caused by an infection with the protozoan parasite Toxoplasma gondii in utero. This disease is characterised by chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, anaemia, or neurological deficits that develop after birth. This disease may present at birth with jaundice, premature birth, hepatosplenomegaly, myocarditis, pneumonitis, or rash. Transmission is by vertical transmission. In the fetus, confirmation is by identification of Toxoplasma gondii in amniotic fluid; in the neonate, confirmation is by identification of Toxoplasma gondii in body fluids or tissues, or detection of antibodies against Toxoplasma gondii.
Index Terms: Congenital toxoplasmosis; Congenital toxoplasma encephalitis; congenital encephalitis due to Toxoplasma gondii; congenital encephalitis in toxoplasmosis; Hydrocephalus due to congenital toxoplasmosis

title: Congenital malaria
Congenital falciparum malaria
Description: A disease caused by an infection with the protozoan parasite Plasmodium falciparum in utero. This disease is characterised by fever, anaemia, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, jaundice, regurgitation, diarrhoea, or poor feeding. This disease may also present with respiratory distress, drowsiness, or cyanosis. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of the Plasmodium falciparum in a blood sample from the neonate.
Index Terms: Congenital falciparum malaria

title: Congenital non-falciparum malaria
Description: Congenital malaria due to Plasmodium species other than Plasmodium falciparum. For example, Plasmodium vivax infects fetuses transplacentally, causing congenital malaria that can lead to anaemia and low birth weight.
Index Terms: Congenital non-falciparum malaria; Congenital malaria due to Plasmodium vivax; Congenital malaria due to Plasmodium ovale

title: Neonatal infections of certain specified sites
Index Terms: Neonatal infections of certain specified sites

title: Neonatal conjunctivitis or dacryocystitis
Description: This refers to inflammation of the conjunctiva (the outermost layer of the eye and the inner surface of the eyelids) and the inflammation of the nasolacrimal sac, frequently caused by nasolacrimal duct obstruction or infection.
Exclusions: Gonococcal conjunctivitis

title: Omphalitis of newborn
Description: A disease of the umbilical cord affecting newborns, commonly caused by an infection with a bacterial source. This disease is characterised by purulent or foul-smelling discharge from the umbilicus or umbilical stump, periumbilical erythema, oedema, or tenderness. This disease may also present with fever, hypothermia, jaundice, tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnoea, respiratory distress, apnoea, or abdominal distention with absent bowel sounds. Transmission is by vertical transmission or iatrogenic transmission. Confirmation is by identification of the infectious agent.
Index Terms: Omphalitis of newborn; Navel infection in newborn; Navel inflammation in newborn; Umbilical infection of newborn; Umbilical stump infection of the newborn; septic umbilical cord; Neonatal staphylococcal omphalitis; Neonatal streptococcal omphalitis; Polymicrobial neonatal omphalitis

title: Neonatal urinary tract infection
Description: A condition of the urinary tract affecting neonates, commonly caused by an infection with a bacterial source. This condition is characterised by fever, pyuria, jaundice, poor appetite, diarrhoea, blood tinged stool, vomiting, or abdominal distention. This condition may also be asymptomatic. Transmission is by vertical transmission. Confirmation is by identification of the infectious agent in a urine sample.
Index Terms: Neonatal urinary tract infection

title: Neonatal infectious mastitis
Description: A disease of the breasts in neonates, may be caused by a maternal infection with a bacterial source. This disease is characterised by swelling, erythema, warmth, tenderness, induration of the breast, or purulent discharge from the nipple. It is usually unilateral. This disease may also present with breast abscesses.
Exclusions: Breast engorgement of newborn; noninfective mastitis of newborn

title: Fetal blood loss
Description: Fetal blood loss is a loss of blood from the fetal circulation during pregnancy, labour, or delivery. Due to the small volume of fetal blood that is present, even a small loss can lead to anaemia or fetal death.

title: Fetal blood loss from vasa praevia
Description: In vasa praevia, the fetal blood vessels connecting the placenta and umbilical cord cross the internal cervical os, the entrance to the birth canal, underneath the fetus. These vessels are more liable to be injured by instrumentation (e.g. scalp electrode, intrauterine pressure catheter, vaginal exams) or simply due to the normal mechanical forces of labour leading to serious fetal blood loss.
Index Terms: Fetal blood loss from vasa praevia; fetal haemorrhage from vasa previa; vasa previa haemorrhage affecting fetus or newborn

title: Fetal blood loss from ruptured cord
Description: The umbilical cord can rupture during labour and delivery and lead to fetal blood loss. Possible reasons include: traction on an abnormally short cord, a cord that is entangled around the fetus, a thin friable cord, or a cord with vascular abnormalities. Fetal blood loss can also occur following accidental cord puncture during amniocentesis or following in utero cordocentesis or transfusion.
Index Terms: Fetal blood loss from ruptured cord; fetal haemorrhage from ruptured cord

title: Fetal blood loss from placenta
Description: Fetal blood loss can result from placental abruption, which is when the placenta separates from the uterine wall prior to delivery. Placental abruptions occur under conditions of maternal hypertension and drug use (e.g. cocaine), maternal vascular and collagen vascular disease, maternal clotting disorders, and following direct abdominal trauma. Fetal blood loss can also result from accidental incision of the placenta during a caesarean section.
Index Terms: Fetal blood loss from placenta; fetal haemorrhage from placenta

title: Haemorrhage into co-twin
Description: Monozygous twins often share a placenta. Vascular anastomoses within the placenta allow for the transfer of blood between the two fetuses. In some cases, the flow is unbalanced and one fetus (larger, plethoric, and polycythaemic twin) has an overload of fluid, while the other (smaller, hydropic twin) becomes anaemic.
Index Terms: Haemorrhage into co-twin; fetal haemorrhage into co-twin

title: Haemorrhage into maternal circulation
Description: Fetal-maternal haemorrhage occurs when the trophoblastic lining of the placenta fails to act as a barrier and allows fetal blood cells to enter the maternal circulation.
Index Terms: Haemorrhage into maternal circulation; fetal haemorrhage into maternal circulation; fetal-maternal haemorrhage

title: Fetal blood loss from cut end of co-twin cord
Description: Sometimes the blood content of monozygotic twins differs considerably, which can occur when anastomoses exist. One twin loses blood through shunts, while the other gains a large quantity of blood. Therefore, following the delivery of one twin, the other may bleed through these anastomoses if the umbilical cord of the delivered twin is not immediately clamped.
Index Terms: Fetal blood loss from cut end of co-twin cord; fetal haemorrhage from cut end of co-twin's cord

title: Umbilical haemorrhage of newborn
Description: A condition characterised by bleeding from the umbilical cord stump of a newborn.
Exclusions: omphalitis with mild haemorrhage

title: Neonatal kernicterus
Description: Kernicterus is a pathologic diagnosis of the neonate that is characterised by yellow staining of the basal ganglia following elevated bilirubin concentrations in the blood and/or a breech in the blood brain barrier more common in the premature infant or the sick term neonate. title: Neonatal vitamin K deficiency
Description: There are 3 forms of vitamin K–deficiency bleeding (VKDB) of the newborn. Early VKDB (haemorrhagic disease of the newborn) that occurs at 1-14 days of age. The most common sites of bleeding are the gastrointestinal tract, mucosal and cutaneous tissue, the umbilical stump, and the post-circumcision site. Late VKDB most commonly occurs at 2-12 weeks of age, although cases can occur up to 6 months. The most common site of bleeding is intracranial, although cutaneous and gastrointestinal bleeding may be initial manifestation. The third form of VKDB occurs at birth or shortly thereafter. It is secondary to maternal intake of medications (warfarin, phenobarbital, phenytoin) that cross the placenta.

title: Diffuse bleeding diathesis due to vitamin K deficient haemorrhagic disease of fetus or newborn

title: Respiratory distress syndrome of newborn
Description: Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) is an acute illness, usually of preterm infants, due to pulmonary surfactant deficiency, developing within 4-6 hours of birth, and is characterised by respiratory distress (tachypnoea, intercostal and sternal retractions, expiratory grunt, and cyanosis) with abnormal chest radiograph showing diffuse reticulogranular densities and air bronchograms, evidence of reduced lung compliance and functional residual capacity, evidence of abnormal gas exchange (hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, cyanosis) of sufficient severity to require oxygen and/or continuous or intermittent positive pressure ventilatory support for more than 24 hours.

title: Respiratory distress syndrome of the newborn, altered by maternal corticosteroid therapy

title: Respiratory distress syndrome of the newborn, altered by pulmonary surfactant replacement therapy

title: Transient tachypnoea of newborn
Description: Transient tachypnoea of newborn is usually a benign self-limiting illness of term and near-term infants demonstrating increased respiratory rate and requiring supplementary oxygen after birth.

title: Respiratory instability of prematurity
Description: Infant within the neonatal period who requires continued respiratory life support, including positive pressure ventilation and/or prolonged oxygen therapy, without a clear pathologic diagnosis. title: Congenital pneumonia

title: Neonatal tracheitis
Description: A disease of the trachea in neonates, caused by an infection with a bacterial, viral, or fungal source. This disease is characterised by stridor, or increased respiratory effort. Transmission is commonly by inhalation of the infectious agent. Confirmation is by direct laryngoscopy.

title: Neonatal aspiration syndromes

title: Neonatal aspiration of meconium

title: Tracheobronchial haemorrhage originating in the perinatal period

title: Chronic respiratory disease originating in the perinatal period
Description: A group of conditions associated with the respiratory system which begin during the period of time around childbirth, especially the five months before and one month after birth, and which lasts for at least 3 months.

title: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia originating in the perinatal period
Description: Chronic lung disease requiring treatment with oxygen for at least 28 days and with a spectrum of severity from mild to severe, that predominantly affects premature infants.

title: Apnoea of newborn
Description: Any condition characterised by suspension of external breathing in a newborn (premature or term) which is not classified elsewhere

title: Central neonatal apnoea
Description: Central apnoea is a cessation of airflow > 20 seconds with loss of all respiratory effort. It is due to immaturity of the brainstem to control respiration. It is found in many premature infants and generally resolves by 36 weeks of age.
Index Terms: Central neonatal apnoea

title: Obstructive neonatal apnoea
Description: Apnoea that occurs secondary to diminished airway airflow from an obstruction in the airway from the nose and mouth, tongue, hypopharynx, epiglottis, vocal cords or subglottic region. This is characterised by initial increased work of breathing and rapid progression to cyanosis.
Index Terms: Obstructive neonatal apnoea; obstructive apnoea of newborn

title: Mixed neonatal apnoea
Description: A combination of central apnoea and obstructive apnoea. Most apnoea of prematurity is of the mixed variety, and most often resolves by 36 weeks of age.
Index Terms: Mixed neonatal apnoea

title: Apnoea of newborn, due to neurologic injury
Index Terms: Apnoea of newborn, due to neurologic injury; Apnoea of newborn secondary to neurologic injury

title: Primary atelectasis of newborn
Description: Failure of the lungs to expand after birth, as in stillborn infants or in liveborn infants who die before respiration is established
Inclusions: Primary failure to expand terminal respiratory units

title: Cyanotic attacks of newborn
Description: Sudden attacks of cyanosis, lasting from a few moments up to half an hour, in an infant whose colour was previously normal, and whose colour returns to normal in atmospheric air after the attack
Exclusions: Apnoea of newborn

title: Acquired vocal cord paralysis in newborn

title: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn

Description: A paediatric condition characterised by an imbalance between the oxygen supply and demand of the heart muscle (myocardium) in a newborn.

title: Neonatal hypertension

Description: Mean Arterial Blood Pressure below gestational age in weeks (corresponds with 10th centile for birth weight and postnatal age 1) or below 30 mmHg as hypotension.

title: Benign or innocent cardiac murmurs in newborn
Description: A paediatric condition characterised by heart sounds that are produced as a result of turbulent blood flow that is sufficient to produce audible noise primarily due to physiologic conditions outside the heart, as opposed to structural defects in the heart itself in a newborn.
